subQueries.subQuery: dataSource.name = 'Cisco Firewall Threat Defense' and event.type = 'Intrusion event' and status_detail = 'Attempted Information Leak'

description: Detects multiple attempts to exfiltrate sensitive information or data through the network, as identified by Cisco Secure Firewall Threat Defense (FTD). Repeated information leak attempts may indicate reconnaissance, automated data exfiltration, or insider threat activity. Monitoring for such patterns helps identify potential breaches and prevent unauthorized disclosure of critical information.
name: Cisco FTD Repeated Attempts of Information Leak

description: Detects a successful privilege escalation activity identified by Cisco Secure Firepower Threat Defense (FTD) where an account has gained elevated privileges (administrator or user). Such activity may indicate that an adversary has successfully exploited a vulnerability, misconfiguration, or credential compromise to obtain higher-level access, enabling lateral movement, system modifications, or execution of sensitive operations. Monitoring these events helps identify confirmed compromises and potential insider threats.
name: Cisco FTD Successful Privilege Escalation
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cisco Firewall Threat Defense' and event.type = 'Intrusion event' and status_detail in ('Successful Administrator Privilege Gain', 'Successful User Privilege Gain')

description: Detects network connections initiated by the Background Intelligent Transfer Service (BITS) as identified by Cisco Secure Firewall Threat Defense (FTD). Adversaries may abuse BITS to stealthily download or upload malicious payloads, evade detection, and maintain persistence by leveraging this trusted Windows service. BITS traffic can indicate suspicious file transfer activity associated with malware delivery or command-and-control communications.
name: Cisco FTD Suspicious BITS Network Activity
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cisco Firewall Threat Defense' and event.type = 'Open' and unmapped.Client contains 'BITS' and not (url.url_string contains '//msedge.b.tlu.dl') and not (unmapped.WebApplication in ('Microsoft Update', 'Microsoft'))

description: Detects file download activity identified by Cisco Secure Firewall Threat Defense (FTD) where high-risk or executable file types are transferred over non-standard ports. Adversaries may leverage uncommon ports to evade network security controls and deliver malicious payloads such as executables, scripts, installers, or shortcut files. Monitoring this behavior can help identify malware delivery, command-and-control staging, or covert data transfer attempts.
name: Cisco FTD Suspicious File Download Over Non-Standard Port
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cisco Firewall Threat Defense' and event.type = 'File events' and unmapped.FileDirection = 'Download' and not (dst_endpoint.port in (443, 80, 445)) and unmapped.FileType in:anycase ('MSEXE', 'LNK', 'ISHIELD_MSI', 'BINHEX', 'BINARY_DATA', 'ELF', 'MACHO', 'JARPACK', 'TORRENT', 'AUTORUN', 'EICAR', 'SCR', 'UNIX_SCRIPT') and unmapped.FileSandboxStatus != 'File Size Is Too Small' and not (unmapped.WebApplication in:anycase ('Microsoft', 'Microsoft Update')) and src_endpoint.port != 445

description: Detects network connections identified as TOR traffic by Cisco Secure Firewall Threat Defense (FTD). The Tor network is commonly used to anonymize network activity and evade monitoring controls. Adversaries may leverage Tor to conceal command-and-control communications, exfiltrate data, or bypass network security restrictions. Monitoring Tor connections can indicate policy violations, anonymization attempts, or malicious activity and should be investigated to determine intent and risk.
name: Cisco FTD TOR Network Connection Detected
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Cisco Firewall Threat Defense' and event.type = 'Open' and status contains 'Allow' and (unmapped.Client = 'TOR' or unmapped.ApplicationProtocol = 'TOR')

description: Detects 10 or more Block events involving the same file hash executed by a standard user in a span of 10 minutes. Multiple execution attempts of an identical binary indicate persistence, automated retry behavior, or scripted execution attempts following enforcement denial. Attack tools and malware commonly retry execution after being blocked, especially when attempting privilege escalation or persistence. Legitimate users typically do not repeatedly execute the same blocked binary within a short timeframe. This detection helps identify potential brute-force execution attempts, staging activity, or misuse of unauthorized tools.
name: CyberArk EPM Multiple Execution Attempts from Blocked File

description: Detects repeated AttackAttempt or SuspiciousActivityAttempt events associated with the same process file hash in a 10 minute window. When a single executable repeatedly triggers privileged threat protection mechanisms, it may indicate automated credential probing, scripted post-exploitation activity, or persistent interaction with protected resources. Legitimate software typically does not continuously generate privileged threat attempts under normal operation. Recurrent activity from the same binary suggests potential misuse, malicious tooling, or repeated attempts to access restricted assets. This detection provides visibility into binaries exhibiting sustained suspicious behavior across one or more endpoints.
name: CyberArk EPM Multiple Threat Protection Events From Same Process

description: Detects a device repeatedly connecting to external or internal hosts. This is commonly seen during botnet compromise activity. An adversary can abuse this behavior by establishing a Command and Control (C2) channel where the compromised endpoint periodically connects back to an attacker-controlled server to check for new commands, download additional stages, or exfiltrate data.
name: Darktrace Beacon Activity Detected
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Darktrace' and category_name = 'Findings' and event.type = 'Create' and unmapped.model.then.name contains 'Beacon'

description: Detects attempts to delete Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) connection history files or related registry entries via command-line utilities such as 'powershell.exe' or 'cmd.exe' on Windows endpoints. This rule identifies process or script executions that invoke deletion commands targeting artifacts like 'Default.rdp', 'Microsoft/Terminal Server Client/Servers', or RDP jump list files. Attackers often use these techniques to erase evidence of RDP usage and evade forensic analysis. Monitoring for these actions helps identify potential lateral movement, anti-forensics activity, or post-exploitation cleanup on compromised systems.
name: Delete RDP History via Command Line Execution
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' AND endpoint.os = 'windows' AND event.type in ('Process Creation', 'Command Script') AND (src.process.name in:anycase ('powershell.exe', 'cmd.exe') OR src.process.displayName in:anycase ('Windows PowerShell', 'Windows Command Processor')) AND (src.process.cmdline contains ('Remove-Item ', 'Remove-ItemProperty ', 'del ', 'delete ', 'erase ', ' rd ', ' ri ', ' rm ', 'rmdir ') OR cmdScript.content contains ('Remove-Item ', 'Remove-ItemProperty ', 'del ', 'delete ', 'erase ', ' rd ', ' ri ', ' rm ', 'rmdir ')) AND (src.process.cmdline contains ('Default.rdp', '\\Microsoft\\Terminal Server Client\\Servers', '\\Microsoft\\Terminal Server Client\\Default', '%AppData%\\Microsoft\\Windows\\Recent\\AutomaticDestinations') OR cmdScript.content contains ('Default.rdp', '\\Microsoft\\Terminal Server Client\\Servers', '\\Microsoft\\Terminal Server Client\\Default', '%AppData%\\Microsoft\\Windows\\Recent\\AutomaticDestinations'))

description: Detects presence of the Doppelganger tool used for dumping credentials from the LSASS process. This activity is indicative of credential access attempts, a common objective in post-exploitation stages by adversaries seeking lateral movement or privilege escalation. The use of specialized LSASS dumping tools aligns with advanced threat actor tactics to extract sensitive authentication material from memory. Such behavior poses a significant risk to enterprise security by enabling unauthorized access to privileged accounts.
name: Doppelganger LSASS Credential Dumper Tool
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type in ('Process Creation', 'Command Script', 'File Creation', 'File Modification') and (src.process.image.originalFileName in:anycase ('Doppelganger.exe') or src.process.name in:anycase ('Doppelganger.exe') or tgt.process.name in:anycase ('Doppelganger.exe') or src.process.cmdline contains 'Doppelganger.exe' or tgt.process.cmdline contains 'Doppelganger.exe' or cmdScript.content contains 'Doppelganger.exe' or tgt.file.OriginalFileName in:anycase ('Doppelganger.exe') or tgt.file.name in:anycase ('Doppelganger.exe') or tgt.file.path contains 'Doppelganger.exe')

description: Detects the creation or modification of Kubernetes resources with host namespace access within Amazon EKS clusters. This detection specifically monitors for pods, deployments, daemonsets, jobs, replicasets, statefulsets, cronjobs, and replication controllers where hostIPC=true, hostPID=true, or hostNetwork=true has been configured. These privileged settings effectively break container isolation by allowing containers to: (1) access the host's inter-process communication mechanisms (hostIPC), (2) view and interact with all processes running on the host system (hostPID), or (3) directly access the host's network interfaces and bind to privileged ports (hostNetwork). Adversaries leverage these configurations to escape container boundaries, perform lateral movement, access sensitive information on the host, intercept network traffic, or manipulate host processes. This technique aligns with MITRE ATT&CK's Privilege Escalation and Container Escape tactics and represents a critical security risk that could lead to complete host compromise. This detection excludes system-initiated changes to focus on potentially unauthorized or malicious activity.
name: EKS Container Created with Host Namespace Access
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'EKS Logs' and http_request.http_method in ('create', 'update', 'patch') and ((unmapped.responseObject.spec.template.spec.hostIPC = true or unmapped.requestObject.spec.template.spec.hostIPC = true or unmapped.responseObject.spec.hostIPC = true or unmapped.responseObject.spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec.hostIPC = true) or (unmapped.responseObject.spec.template.spec.hostPID = true or unmapped.requestObject.spec.template.spec.hostPID = true or unmapped.responseObject.spec.hostPID = true or unmapped.responseObject.spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec.hostPID = true) or (unmapped.responseObject.spec.template.spec.hostNetwork = true or unmapped.requestObject.spec.template.spec.hostNetwork = true or unmapped.responseObject.spec.hostNetwork = true or unmapped.responseObject.spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec.hostNetwork = true)) and not (actor.user.name matches '^(system|eks):')

description: Detects the deletion or modification of HorizontalPodAutoscaler resources within an Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) cluster. HorizontalPodAutoscalers are Kubernetes resources that automatically scale the number of pod replicas based on observed CPU utilization or other select metrics. While configuration changes to HorizontalPodAutoscalers are part of normal operations, unauthorized modifications could lead to resource availability issues or performance degradation. Adversaries may target these resources to disrupt autoscaling functionality, potentially causing service outages during high traffic periods or enabling resource hijacking by manipulating scaling thresholds. By modifying or deleting HorizontalPodAutoscaler configurations, attackers can impact application performance, cause denial of service, or increase cloud resource consumption resulting in financial damage. Monitoring these events helps identify potential security threats and maintain the stability and efficiency of your Kubernetes environment.
name: EKS HorizontalPodAutoscaler Configuration Modified or Deleted
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'EKS Logs' and http_request.http_method in ('delete', 'patch', 'update') and not (actor.user.name matches '^(system|eks):') and resources.type = 'horizontalpodautoscalers'

description: Detects attempts to modify or delete audit logs on ESXi hosts via esxcli. Such activity may indicate efforts by adversaries to tamper with forensic evidence or conceal unauthorized actions within the virtualized environment. Attackers often target audit logs to disrupt incident response and hinder detection of malicious operations. This behavior is associated with defense evasion tactics and poses a significant risk to the integrity of security monitoring on ESXi infrastructure.
name: ESXi Audit Log Configuration Modification
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'linux' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and tgt.process.name = 'esxcli' and tgt.process.cmdline contains ' config' and tgt.process.cmdline contains ' syslog' and tgt.process.cmdline contains ' set'

description: Detects changes to the ESXi firewall configuration outside of standard administrative operations via esxcli. Unauthorized or unexpected modifications to firewall settings may indicate an attempt to weaken host defenses or facilitate lateral movement within the virtualized environment. Adversaries may alter firewall rules to enable malicious access or exfiltration of data. Monitoring for such activity is critical to maintaining the integrity and security posture of ESXi hosts.
name: ESXi Firewall Configuration Modification
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'linux' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and tgt.process.name = 'esxcli' and tgt.process.cmdline contains ' network' and tgt.process.cmdline contains ' firewall' and not (tgt.process.cmdline contains (' list', ' get'))

description: Detects creation, modification, or deletion of user accounts on ESXi hosts using the esxcli. Unauthorized changes to ESXi user accounts may indicate adversary attempts to establish persistence, escalate privileges, or disrupt operations within the virtualized environment. Such activity is often associated with lateral movement or privilege escalation tactics. Monitoring these events is critical for identifying potential compromise or misuse of administrative access on ESXi infrastructure.
name: ESXi User Account Management Activity
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'linux' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and tgt.process.name = 'esxcli' and tgt.process.cmdline contains ' system' and tgt.process.cmdline contains ' account' and not (tgt.process.cmdline contains ' list')

description: Detects the execution of non-native or third-party SSH client processes on endpoints. Such activity may indicate attempts to bypass standard security controls, establish unauthorized remote access, or evade monitoring solutions. Adversaries may leverage alternative SSH clients to blend in with legitimate traffic or circumvent endpoint restrictions. Monitoring for non-standard SSH client usage helps identify potential lateral movement or data exfiltration tactics within the environment.
name: Execution of Non-Standard SSH Client Applications
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'linux' and event.type = 'IP Connect' and event.network.protocolName = 'ssh' and ((src.process.name = 'remmina' and src.process.cmdline contains ('-t', '-c') and src.process.cmdline contains 'ssh') or (src.process.name = 'plink' and src.process.cmdline contains ('-i', '-l', '-pw', '@', '-s')) or (src.process.name = 'termius' and src.process.cmdline contains ('connect', 'login')) or (src.process.name = 'mosh' and src.process.cmdline contains ('@', 'ssh', '-i')) or (src.process.name = 'tinyssh-client' and src.process.cmdline contains ('-u', '-i')) or (src.process.name = 'dbclient' and src.process.cmdline contains ('@', '-l', '-i')))

description: Detects the combination of curl and osacompile commands, which may indicate an adversary downloading AppleScript payloads and compiling them locally on macOS. While curl is a legitimate tool for retrieving content from the web, and osacompile is used to compile AppleScript scripts, their combined use can signal malicious activity such as script-based persistence, payload execution, or evasion. This technique enables attackers to fetch and transform remote scripts into executable formats on the target system, bypassing static detection mechanisms. Monitoring this behavior helps identify living-off-the-land tactics commonly leveraged in macOS-focused intrusions.
name: File Download and Compile via osacompile
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'osx' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and tgt.process.name contains 'curl' and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'osacompile'

subQueries.subQuery: event.type = 'Command Script' AND cmdScript.content contains 'Net.Sockets.TCPClient' AND cmdScript.content contains ('[IO.FileMode]::Create', '::WriteAllBytes', '::WriteAllText', '.Write(', 'IO.Compression.GzipStream')
event.type = 'IP Connect' AND src.process.activeContent.signedStatus = 'unsigned' AND NOT (dst.ip.address matches '^(10\\\.|192\\\.168\\\.|172\\\.(1[6-9]|2[0-9]|3[0-1])\\\.)')

description: Detects potential fileless data exfiltration activity where unsigned processes exhibit behaviors commonly associated with advanced adversary techniques. This includes the use of obfuscated or encoded commands to evade detection, suspicious discovery actions that may indicate reconnaissance or identification of sensitive data, and attempts to access or modify decoy files designed to lure attackers. Additionally, the rule monitors for unsigned processes initiating outbound network connections to external IP addresses, which may signal attempts to transfer data outside the organization without leaving traditional file artifacts on disk. Such behaviors are indicative of sophisticated attacks that leverage in-memory execution and living-off-the-land tactics to bypass conventional security controls, posing a significant risk of data loss and unauthorized disclosure. Please enable the 'SuspiciousDiscoveryByAI' Behavioral indicator to make this rule work.
name: Fileless Data Exfiltration via Unsigned Process

description: Detects the execution of a command that programmatically disables the system's firewall by modifying its configuration settings to turn off protection. Disabling the firewall in this manner can be a deliberate action by an adversary seeking to weaken the system's security posture and remove a critical layer of defense. By turning off the firewall, the attacker can facilitate unrestricted network communication, making it easier to establish unauthorized connections, move laterally within the environment, or exfiltrate sensitive data without being blocked or detected by standard network controls. This behavior is often associated with attempts to evade detection, deploy additional malicious payloads, or maintain persistence on the compromised host. The ability to disable firewall protections through scriptable means is a powerful technique for adversaries, as it can be automated and executed remotely, increasing the risk of widespread compromise across multiple systems. Detecting such activity is crucial for identifying early stages of an attack where the adversary is preparing the environment for further malicious operations.
name: Firewall Disabled via PowerShell
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Command Script' and ((src.process.name in:anycase ('powershell.exe') or src.process.displayName = 'Windows PowerShell')) and cmdScript.content contains 'Set-NetFirewallProfile' and cmdScript.content matches '\\s-Enabled\\s+False\\s'

description: Detects the first instance of disabling the iptables or firewall service using the systemctl utility by a user in a 24 hours window . Disabling firewall protections can be abused by attackers to bypass network controls, gain unauthorized access, and conduct malicious activities, posing significant risks to system security.
name: First Seen Firewall Disabled via Systemctl Utility

description: Detects the first instance of a firewall rule being deleted in Google Cloud Platform (GCP) for Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) or App Engine by a specific user identity within a 7-day window. Firewall rules are essential for enforcing network security policies, and unauthorized deletions can expose cloud environments to potential threats, such as unauthorized access or malicious traffic. Monitoring this activity helps identify potential misconfigurations, privilege misuse, or security breaches.
name: First Seen GCP Firewall Deletion

description: Detects the initial occurrence of a successful login via Palo Alto Networks GlobalProtect originating from a previously unseen country within a 7-day period. Such activity may signify anomalous behavior, as adversaries often exploit compromised credentials to access accounts from unusual locations. This pattern is commonly associated with account takeover attempts or unauthorized remote access operations.
name: First Seen PANW Firewall GlobalProtect Login from a Country

description: Detects the first instance of LDAP traffic to an external IP in a 7 day window. LDAP traffic is commonly used for directory services within an organization. LDAP traffic to public IP addresses could indicate data exfiltration attempts by malicious actors.
name: First Seen PANW Firewall LDAP Traffic to Public IP

description: Detects the first instance of Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) traffic originating from a country in a 7 day window. This rule highlights potentially anomalous activity. An adversary can utilize this to establish unauthorized remote access, indicating a possible system compromise or an attempt to bypass geographical restrictions, potentially leading to data exfiltration or further malicious actions.
name: First Seen PANW Firewall RDP Connection from a Country

description: Detects the first instance of SMB traffic to an external IP in a 7 day window. Adversaries can abuse the SMB protocol to transfer large volumes of data from compromised internal systems to external servers under their control. This exfiltration can be performed covertly and may involve compressing or encrypting the data to evade detection.
name: First Seen PANW Firewall SMB Traffic to Public IP

description: Detects the creation of a new administrator user account on a Fortinet FortiGate device originating from a public IP address. An adversary who gains access to the management interface may create unauthorized admin accounts to establish persistent, privileged control over the firewall. By creating these accounts from external or atypical network locations, attackers can maintain long-term access, modify security policies, exfiltrate sensitive data, or prepare the environment for additional malicious activity.
name: FortiGate Admin User Created from Public IP

description: Detects the virus in the network identified by FortiGate Firewall. This may indicate the presence of malware or a malicious file attempting to execute or transfer within the network. Threat actors may use malware to gain access, maintain persistence, or exfiltrate data. Monitoring such events can help identify compromised systems or prevent further infection spread.
name: FortiGate Firewall Virus Detected
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'FortiGate' and event.type = 'virus' and unmapped.eventtype in ('infected', '0-day-malware-stream', 'exempt-hash', 'inline-block', 'malware-list', 'mimefragmented', 'unknown-ce') and unmapped.level != 'information' and not (unmapped.action in ('blocked', 'attachment-removed')) and not (status_detail in ('blocked', 'attachment-removed'))

description: Detects potential exploitation attempts targeting CVE-2025-64446, a critical path traversal vulnerability affecting Fortinet FortiWeb Web Application Firewalls (WAF). An adversary can abuse this flaw, which requires no authentication, to create new, unauthorized administrative user accounts on the exposed device. This provides the threat actor with full administrative control over the security appliance, allowing them to bypass security policies, neutralize the WAF, and establish a persistent backdoor for further network intrusions.
name: FortiGate FortiWeb Path Traversal Vulnerability Exploitation Attempt
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'FortiGate' and http_request.http_method = 'POST' and unmapped.url matches '(?:\\/system\\/admin%3F\\/)(?:(?:\\.\\.\\/)+)(?:cgi-bin\\/fwbcgi)' and not (unmapped.action in ('blocked', 'block', 'reset', 'drop', 'dropped'))

description: Detects attempts to download a FortiGate configuration file from an external or publicly accessible network source. Adversaries may abuse this behavior to obtain sensitive configuration data, including administrative credentials, network topology details, VPN settings, or firewall policies. Access to this information can enable further compromise through targeted lateral movement, privilege escalation, or tailored exploitation of exposed services.
name: FortiGate Suspicious Config File Access from External Network

description: Detects instances where the FortiGate Web Application Firewall (WAF) has blocked HTTP requests due to violations of configured HTTP protocol constraints. While such blocks may result from legitimate misconfigurations or user errors, repeated or unexpected occurrences could indicate attempts to exploit protocol weaknesses, bypass security controls, or conduct reconnaissance. Monitoring these events helps identify potential web application attacks and ensures adherence to security policies.
name: FortiGate WAF Blocked Application by HTTP Constraints
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'FortiGate' and unmapped.eventtype = 'waf-http-constraint' and unmapped.action = 'blocked'

description: Detects instances where the FortiGate Web Application Firewall (WAF) blocks application access based on entries in a configured address list. While this may be part of normal security operations, repeated or unexpected blocks could indicate attempted access from unauthorized or malicious sources. Monitoring these events helps identify potential threats, enforce access policies, and maintain the integrity of protected web applications.
name: FortiGate WAF Blocked Application via Address List
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'FortiGate' and unmapped.eventtype = 'waf-address-list' and unmapped.action = 'blocked'

description: Detects instances where the FortiGate Web Application Firewall (WAF) has blocked application traffic based on a custom signature. This may indicate an attempt to exploit a known or organization-specific vulnerability, unauthorized access, or malicious activity targeting web applications. Monitoring these events helps identify and respond to tailored attack patterns and enhances the security posture of protected web assets.
name: FortiGate WAF Blocked Application via Custom Signature
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'FortiGate' and unmapped.eventtype = 'waf-custom-signature' and unmapped.action = 'blocked'

description: Detects instances where the FortiGate Web Application Firewall (WAF) has blocked an application request based on the HTTP method used. This may indicate attempts to exploit vulnerabilities or bypass security controls using disallowed or suspicious HTTP methods. Monitoring these events helps identify potential web application attacks and enforce proper HTTP method usage.
name: FortiGate WAF Blocked Application via HTTP method
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'FortiGate' and unmapped.eventtype = 'waf-http-method' and unmapped.action = 'blocked'

description: Detects instances where the FortiGate Web Application Firewall (WAF) has blocked access to an application based on URL filtering policies. This may indicate attempts to access restricted or malicious URLs, potential policy violations, or unauthorized access attempts. Monitoring these events helps identify possible security threats, enforce compliance, and protect web applications from exploitation.
name: FortiGate WAF Blocked Application via URL Access
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'FortiGate' and unmapped.eventtype = 'waf-url-access' and unmapped.action = 'blocked'

description: Detects a super admin login attempt to a FortiGate firewall originating from a suspicious or public IP address. This may indicate an attempt to exploit CVE-2025-24472 which allows unauthenticated attackers to gain super admin privileges on vulnerable FortiOS devices (<7.0.16) with exposed management interfaces.
name: Fortinet FortiGate Suspicious Super Admin Login Detected
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'FortiGate' and event.type = 'system' and unmapped.logdesc = 'Admin login successful' and unmapped.profile = 'super_admin' and unmapped.method = 'jsconsole' and status_detail = 'success' and not (unmapped.srcip matches '^(10\\.|192\\.168\\.|172\\.(1[6-9]|2[0-9]|3[0-1])\\.)')

description: Detects the creation of a Web Application Firewall (WAF) security policy in Google Cloud Armor. Adversaries may create WAF policies to bypass security controls, manipulate traffic filtering rules, or establish persistence within the cloud environment. Unauthorized WAF policy creation can lead to reduced protection against web-based threats, increased attack surface, or evasion of existing security measures. Monitoring WAF security policy creation is crucial for maintaining web application security, detecting potential threats, and preventing misconfigurations that could expose applications to exploitation.
name: GCP Cloud Armor WAF Security Policy Creation
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'GCP Audit' and api.service.name = 'compute.googleapis.com' and unmapped.protoPayload.methodName matches 'compute.securityPolicies.insert$' and unmapped.severity != 'ERROR'

description: Detects the deletion of a Web Application Firewall (WAF) security policy in Google Cloud Armor. name: GCP Cloud Armor WAF Security Policy Deletion

description: Detects the deletion of a rule from a Web Application Firewall (WAF) security policy in Google Cloud Armor. Adversaries may delete WAF rules to weaken security controls, manipulate traffic filtering rules, or establish persistence within the cloud environment. Unauthorized WAF rule deletion can lead to reduced protection against web-based threats, increased attack surface, or evasion of existing security measures. name: GCP Cloud Armor WAF Security Policy Rule Deletion
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'GCP Audit' and api.service.name = 'compute.googleapis.com' and unmapped.protoPayload.methodName matches 'compute.securityPolicies.removeRule$' and unmapped.severity != 'ERROR'

description: Detects the modification of a rule in a Web Application Firewall (WAF) security policy in Google Cloud Armor. s1ql: dataSource.name = 'GCP Audit' and api.service.name = 'compute.googleapis.com' and unmapped.protoPayload.methodName matches 'compute.securityPolicies.patchRule$' and unmapped.severity != 'ERROR'

description: Detects the modification of a Web Application Firewall (WAF) security policy in Google Cloud Armor. Unauthorized WAF policy modification can lead to reduced protection against web-based threats, increased attack surface, or evasion of existing security measures. s1ql: dataSource.name = 'GCP Audit' and api.service.name = 'compute.googleapis.com' and unmapped.protoPayload.methodName matches 'compute.securityPolicies.patch$' and unmapped.severity != 'ERROR'

description: Detects the creation of a firewall rule in Google Cloud Platform (GCP) for Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) or App Engine. Firewall rules control network traffic and dictate which resources can communicate within the cloud environment. Unauthorized creation of firewall rules can expose the environment to security risks by permitting unintended access, facilitating lateral movement, or bypassing established security controls, ultimately compromising the integrity and security of the environment.
name: GCP Firewall Rule Creation
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'GCP Audit' and api.service.name in ('compute.googleapis.com', 'appengine.googleapis.com') and unmapped.protoPayload.methodName matches ('compute.firewalls.insert', 'FirewallPolicies.insert', 'Firewall.Create') and unmapped.severity != 'ERROR'

description: Detects the creation of a firewall rule in GCP that permits egress traffic on TCP port 3389 (RDP) to any IPv4 or IPv6 destination. While RDP is primarily used for inbound remote access, egress rules enabling RDP may signal unusual behavior or attempts to establish unauthorized external connections. Attackers may use such rules to pivot to external infrastructure or connect back to command-and-control servers. Allowing RDP traffic outbound could be a sign of malware attempting to spread or exfiltrate data. Monitoring these changes helps identify misconfigurations and potential post-compromise activity.
name: GCP Firewall Rule Creation to Allow Egress To Any IP Over Any RDP
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'GCP Audit' and api.service.name = 'compute.googleapis.com' and unmapped.protoPayload.methodName matches 'compute.firewalls.(patch|update)$' and unmapped.protoPayload.resourceOriginalState.direction = 'EGRESS' and unmapped.protoPayload.request.destinationRanges in ('[0.0.0.0/0]', '[::/0]') and unmapped.protoPayload.request.alloweds matches 'ports":\\[(.*,)?\\"3389\\"(,.*)?\\]' and unmapped.severity != 'ERROR'

description: Detects the creation of a firewall rule in GCP that enables egress traffic on TCP port 22 (SSH) to any IPv4 or IPv6 destination. Although SSH is typically used for inbound administration, allowing outbound SSH traffic can facilitate lateral movement, data exfiltration, or connections to external systems. Adversaries might abuse such rules to pivot to attacker-controlled infrastructure or exfiltrate stolen credentials. This configuration is uncommon in secure environments and should be reviewed for potential abuse. Monitoring outbound SSH permissions helps detect malicious behavior and enforce network boundaries.
name: GCP Firewall Rule Creation to Allow Egress To Any IP Over Any SSH
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'GCP Audit' and api.service.name = 'compute.googleapis.com' and unmapped.protoPayload.methodName matches 'compute.firewalls.(patch|update)$' and unmapped.protoPayload.resourceOriginalState.direction = 'EGRESS' and unmapped.protoPayload.request.destinationRanges in ('[0.0.0.0/0]', '[::/0]') and unmapped.protoPayload.request.alloweds matches 'ports":\\[(.*,)?\\"22\\"(,.*)?\\]' and unmapped.severity != 'ERROR'

description: Detects the creation of a firewall rule in Google Cloud Platform (GCP) that permits egress traffic to any IPv4 destination over any protocol. Such changes can enable unauthorized data exfiltration, communication with external command-and-control servers, or bypass of network segmentation controls. Adversaries may leverage this misconfiguration to extract sensitive information, download additional malware, or maintain persistent access within the environment. Monitoring firewall rule modifications is essential to uphold a strong security posture and prevent potential breaches.
name: GCP Firewall Rule Creation to Allow Egress To Any IPv4 Over Any Protocol
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'GCP Audit' and api.service.name = 'compute.googleapis.com' and unmapped.protoPayload.methodName matches 'compute.firewalls.(patch|update)$' and unmapped.protoPayload.resourceOriginalState.direction = 'EGRESS' and unmapped.protoPayload.request.destinationRanges = '[0.0.0.0/0]' and unmapped.protoPayload.request.alloweds = '[{"IPProtocol":"all"}]' and unmapped.severity != 'ERROR'

description: Detects the creation of a firewall rule in Google Cloud Platform (GCP) that permits egress traffic to any IPv6 destination over any protocol. Such changes can enable unauthorized data exfiltration, communication with external command-and-control servers, or bypass of network segmentation controls. Adversaries may leverage this misconfiguration to extract sensitive information, download additional malware, or maintain persistent access within the environment. Monitoring firewall rule modifications is essential to uphold a strong security posture and prevent potential breaches.
name: GCP Firewall Rule Creation to Allow Egress To Any IPv6 Over Any Protocol
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'GCP Audit' and api.service.name = 'compute.googleapis.com' and unmapped.protoPayload.methodName matches 'compute.firewalls.insert$' and unmapped.protoPayload.request.direction = 'EGRESS' and unmapped.protoPayload.request.destinationRanges = '[::/0]' and unmapped.protoPayload.request.alloweds = '[{"IPProtocol":"all"}]' and unmapped.severity != 'ERROR'

description: Detects the creation of a firewall rule in Google Cloud Platform (GCP) that allows ingress traffic on TCP port 3389 (Remote Desktop Protocol) from any IPv4 or IPv6 source. Such rules expose RDP services to the public internet, increasing the attack surface and risk of brute-force or exploit-based attacks. Adversaries may use exposed RDP access to gain initial access, move laterally, or maintain persistence. This activity may indicate a misconfiguration or malicious attempt to weaken the network perimeter. Continuous monitoring of firewall rule changes is crucial for reducing exposure to external threats.
name: GCP Firewall Rule Creation to Allow Ingress From Any IP Over RDP
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'GCP Audit' and api.service.name = 'compute.googleapis.com' and unmapped.protoPayload.methodName matches 'compute.firewalls.(patch|update)$' and unmapped.protoPayload.resourceOriginalState.direction = 'INGRESS' and unmapped.protoPayload.request.sourceRanges in ('[0.0.0.0/0]', '[::/0]') and unmapped.protoPayload.request.alloweds matches 'ports":\\[(.*,)?\\"3398\\"(,.*)?\\]' and unmapped.severity != 'ERROR'

description: Detects the creation of a firewall rule in GCP allowing ingress traffic on TCP port 22 (SSH) from any IPv4 or IPv6 source. SSH exposure to the public internet significantly raises the risk of brute-force attacks and unauthorized remote access. Adversaries may exploit this configuration to gain initial access, deploy persistence mechanisms, or escalate privileges. This rule may reflect poor security hygiene or an intentional weakening of network defenses. Regular review of firewall rules is essential to minimize exposure and maintain least-privilege access controls.
name: GCP Firewall Rule Creation to Allow Ingress From Any IP Over SSH
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'GCP Audit' and api.service.name = 'compute.googleapis.com' and unmapped.protoPayload.methodName matches 'compute.firewalls.(patch|update)$' and unmapped.protoPayload.resourceOriginalState.direction = 'INGRESS' and unmapped.protoPayload.request.sourceRanges in ('[0.0.0.0/0]', '[::/0]') and unmapped.protoPayload.request.alloweds matches 'ports":\\[(.*,)?\\"22\\"(,.*)?\\]' and unmapped.severity != 'ERROR'

description: Detects the deletion of a firewall rule in Google Cloud Platform (GCP) for Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) or App Engine. name: GCP Firewall Rule Deletion

Altering firewall rules can impact the flow of network traffic and may compromise security, allowing unauthorized access or malicious activities. Unauthorized or incorrect changes to firewall rules can increase vulnerabilities, potentially exposing critical resources to risk and bypassing security controls.
name: GCP Firewall Rule Modification
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'GCP Audit' and api.service.name in ('compute.googleapis.com', 'appengine.googleapis.com') and unmapped.protoPayload.methodName contains ('compute.firewalls.patch', 'compute.firewalls.update', 'Firewall.Update') and unmapped.severity != 'ERROR' and not (unmapped.protoPayload.authenticationInfo.principalSubject matches 'service-(\\d+)@container-engine-robot\\.iam\\.gserviceaccount\\.com')

Such changes can expose resources to unauthorized access, lateral movement, and exploitation of publicly accessible services. Adversaries may exploit this misconfiguration to compromise critical systems, exfiltrate sensitive data, or escalate malicious activities within the environment.
