description: Detects the enablement or use of full network traffic packet capture in Google Cloud Platform (GCP). Packet capture allows detailed inspection of network traffic and is commonly used for troubleshooting and performance analysis. However, adversaries may abuse packet capture capabilities to intercept sensitive data in transit, monitor internal communications, collect credentials, or conduct reconnaissance within a compromised environment.
name: GCP Network Packet Capture Activity
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'GCP Audit' and api.service.name = 'compute.googleapis.com' and unmapped.protoPayload.methodName matches 'Compute\\.PacketMirrorings\\.(Delete|Get|Insert|List|Patch|aggregatedList)' and unmapped.severity != 'ERROR'

description: Detects the deletion of a topic in Google Cloud Platform (GCP) Pub/Sub service. An adversary can exploit this action to disrupt the flow of messages between services, potentially causing service outages, data loss, or the failure of critical event-driven workflows. Deleting topics can hinder communication between producers and consumers of messages, affecting application functionality and security monitoring.
name: GCP Pub Sub Topic Deletion
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'GCP Audit' and api.service.name = 'pubsub.googleapis.com' and unmapped.protoPayload.methodName matches 'google.pubsub.v..Publisher.DeleteTopic$' and unmapped.severity != 'ERROR'

description: Detects changes to Google Workspace Groups settings that configure the default visibility of group conversations to be accessible by all users within the domain upon group creation. Unauthorized modifications to this setting can lead to security risks, including unauthorized access to sensitive communications and potential data breaches. Monitoring these changes is essential to detect potential security threats and prevent unauthorized access to sensitive information.
name: Google Workspace Default Group Conversation Visibility to Domain Users Enabled
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Google Workspace' and metadata.product.name = 'admin' and metadata.log_name = 'APPLICATION_SETTINGS' and event.type = 'CHANGE_APPLICATION_SETTING' and enrichments contains '"name":"SETTING_NAME","value":"GroupsSharingSettingsProto default_view_topics_access_level"' and enrichments contains '"name":"NEW_VALUE","value":"DOMAIN_USERS"'

description: Detects changes to Google Workspace Groups settings that configure the default visibility of group conversations to be accessible by anyone upon group creation. Unauthorized modifications to this setting can lead to security risks, including unauthorized external access to sensitive communications and potential data breaches. Monitoring these changes is essential to detect potential security threats and prevent unauthorized access to sensitive information.
name: Google Workspace Default Group Conversation Visibility to Public Enabled
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Google Workspace' and metadata.product.name = 'admin' and metadata.log_name = 'APPLICATION_SETTINGS' and event.type = 'CHANGE_APPLICATION_SETTING' and enrichments contains '"name":"SETTING_NAME","value":"GroupsSharingSettingsProto default_view_topics_access_level"' and enrichments contains '"name":"NEW_VALUE","value":"PUBLIC"'

description: Detects instances where multi-factor authentication (MFA) is disabled for user accounts within Google Workspace. MFA adds an extra layer of security by requiring users to verify their identity using multiple factors, such as passwords, security tokens, or biometrics. Disabling MFA increases the risk of unauthorized access, account compromise, and potential data breaches. Monitoring for disabled MFA is essential to detect potential security threats and prevent unauthorized access to sensitive information.
name: Google Workspace Multi-Factor Authentication Disabled
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Google Workspace' and metadata.product.name = 'admin' and event.type in ('ENFORCE_STRONG_AUTHENTICATION', 'ALLOW_STRONG_AUTHENTICATION') and enrichments contains '"name":"NEW_VALUE","value":"false"'

description: Detects the use of Hping, a network tool commonly utilized for diagnostics, testing, and firewall rule auditing on Linux systems. While Hping is a legitimate tool, adversaries can abuse it for various malicious activities, including conducting Denial of Service (DoS) attacks, port scanning, and network reconnaissance. By sending crafted packets, attackers can gather information about a target system's open ports, firewall configuration, and network vulnerabilities. Furthermore, Hping can be used to spoof packets, evade detection, or launch smokescreen attacks to divert attention while conducting other malicious activities.
name: Hping Network Utility Activity
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'linux' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and tgt.process.name in ('hping', 'hping2', 'hping3') and not (src.process.parent.name in:anycase ('nbagent'))

description: Detects instances where HTTP logging in Internet Information Services (IIS) has been disabled. This action may signify an adversary employing anti-forensics tactics to evade detection after gaining unauthorized access to the server, possibly via a web shell or other exploit. Disabling HTTP logging obscures malicious activities, complicating efforts to trace attack vectors, monitor unauthorized access, or assess the scope of a compromise.
name: IIS HTTP Logging Disabled
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (src.process.name in:anycase ('appcmd.exe') or src.process.displayName in:anycase ('appcmd.exe')) and (src.process.cmdline contains '/dontLog:' and src.process.cmdline contains 'true') and not (src.process.parent.name in:anycase ('iissetup.exe') or src.process.displayName in:anycase ('iissetup'))

description: Detects exploitation of the Kubernetes nodes/proxy GET permission, which can enable remote command execution on cluster nodes via direct connections to the Kubelet API. Service accounts with nodes/proxy GET access—often required by monitoring tools for reading Pod metrics and logs—may inadvertently gain the ability to execute arbitrary commands in any Pod, including privileged system Pods, if they reach the Kubelet on port 10250 using connection protocols like WebSockets. This stems from the Kubelet authorizing WebSocket connections based solely on the initial handshake, without enforcing additional CREATE permissions required by the /exec endpoint. Attackers leveraging this misconfiguration can achieve full cluster compromise while evading detection, as Kubernetes AuditPolicy does not log such command executions. The risk is not vendor-specific and is prevalent due to the widespread use of nodes/proxy GET permissions in public Helm charts and vendor solutions. Organizations are advised to review service account permissions, consult vendors, and monitor for suspicious direct Kubelet API interactions.
name: K8s RCE via Nodes/Proxy GET Request
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'linux' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and tgt.process.cmdline contains '10250/exec/' and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'error=1' and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'output=1' and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'command=' and k8sCluster.containerName = *

description: Detects registry-based autorun persistence consistent with Latrodectus masquerading as Intel Graphics Media Accelerator components. This behavior enables durable startup execution while blending with legitimate graphics driver artifacts, aiding defense evasion and facilitating follow-on actions after reboot. Please enable the 'RegistryAutorun' Behavioral indicator to make this rule work.
name: Latrodectus Spoofed Intel Graphics Registry Entry
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Behavioral Indicators' and indicator.name = 'RegistryAutorun' and indicator.metadata matches 'Key: "\\\\registry\\\\machine\\\\Software\\\\Microsoft\\\\Windows\\\\CurrentVersion\\\\(Run|RunOnce)"' and indicator.metadata matches 'ValueData: .*\\\\igfxSDK\\.exe"'

description: Detects potential bypass of security controls by renaming highly relevant binaries. This technique is often employed to disguise malicious activities under the guise of legitimate processes, making it challenging for traditional security measures to identify threats. By monitoring masqueraded process creation events this rule helps uncover attempts to execute renamed binaries that deviate from expected paths. Such evasion tactics are commonly used to maintain stealth and persistence, or perform living off the land application bypass.
name: Masquerading via Renamed System Binaries
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and event.type = 'Behavioral Indicators' and indicator.name = 'DifferentOriginalFilename' and src.process.displayName in ('Microsoft (R) HTML Application host', 'Microsoft ® Console Based Script Host', 'Microsoft ® Windows Based Script Host', 'CertUtil.exe', 'Microsoft Connection Manager Profile Installer', 'IE Per-User Initialization Utility', 'msxsl.exe', 'Execute processes remotely', 'Registry Console Tool', 'Microsoft(C) Register Server', 'Windows host process (Rundll32)', 'Windows Problem Reporting', 'WMI Commandline Utility', 'TCPIP Finger Command') and not (src.process.name in:anycase ('mshta.exe', 'cscript.exe', 'wscript.exe', 'certutil.exe', 'cmstp.exe', 'IE4UINIT.EXE', 'msxsl.exe', 'psexec.exe', 'psexec.c', 'psexec64.exe', 'reg.exe', 'regsvr32.exe', 'upwreg.exe', 'rundll32.exe', 'wermgr.exe', 'WerFault.exe', 'wmic.exe', 'UCPD-fix.exe', 'bssafe.exe', 'finger.exe')) and not (src.process.name matches '^\\$\\$DeleteMe.*$|^cscript-|^jmsagent-|^MSI[A-Za-z0-9]+\\.tmp')

description: Detects the execution of MemProcFS in conjunction with the WinPmem driver (e.g., winpmem_x64.sys). MemProcFS is a memory forensics tool that creates a filesystem representation of physical memory, and WinPmem is a driver commonly used to facilitate memory acquisition. This combination may be used by attackers to collect forensic artifacts, analyze credentials, or bypass endpoint detection.
name: MemProcFS Execution With WinPmem Driver Load
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Driver Load' and src.process.name = 'memprocfs.exe' and tgt.file.path matches '\\\\winpmem_x64\\.sys$'

description: Detects the creation of special files within a containerized environment, which is an uncommon operation in most container workloads. This behavior can be indicative of an attempt to establish device nodes or other special file types that facilitate direct interaction with system hardware or kernel interfaces. Adversaries may abuse this capability to bypass container isolation boundaries, enabling access to sensitive host resources or devices that are not typically exposed to containers. Such actions can be leveraged as part of container escape techniques, where an attacker seeks to gain unauthorized access to the underlying host system or escalate privileges beyond the intended scope of the container. The ability to create special files inside a container can also be used to manipulate device files, potentially leading to data exfiltration, disruption of services, or further compromise of the host environment. This detection highlights activity that may be associated with advanced threat tactics targeting containerized infrastructure.
name: Mknod Execution in Container
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'linux' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and tgt.process.name = 'mknod' and k8sCluster.containerId = * and tgt.process.cmdline contains ' /dev/' and not (tgt.process.cmdline contains ('/dev/xconsole', '/dev/null', '/dev/zero', '/dev/random', '/dev/urandom', '/dev/tty', '/dev/console', '/dev/ptmx', '/dev/loop'))

description: Detects the use of MSHTA to execute JavaScript code from a remote URL, a technique often leveraged by adversaries to deliver and run malicious payloads. This behavior is indicative of initial access or execution tactics, as MSHTA can bypass certain security controls and facilitate code execution without user interaction. Threat actors frequently exploit this method to establish a foothold, download additional malware, or perform further malicious actions on the compromised host.
name: MSHTA Launching Remote JavaScript Payload
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (tgt.process.name in:anycase ('mshta.exe') or tgt.process.displayName = 'Microsoft (R) HTML Application host') and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'javascript:'

description: Detects the deletion of an Oracle Cloud Infrastructure (OCI) Notification Service topic. Deleting topics can disrupt alerting and notification workflows. Unauthorized or unexpected deletion of topics may indicate attempts to suppress alerts or cover tracks, potentially as part of malicious activity targeting the cloud environment. Monitoring topic deletions helps ensure that critical notifications remain intact and any suspicious activity is identified.
name: OCI Notification Topic Deleted
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Oracle Cloud Infrastructure' and event.type = 'DeleteTopic' and unmapped.data.message contains 'DeleteTopic succeeded'

description: Detects attempts to delete or modify Network Security Groups (NSGs) or Security Lists in Oracle Cloud Infrastructure (OCI). Adversaries can abuse this behavior to weaken or completely disable network defenses by removing segmentation, altering access controls, or deleting firewall rules. By tampering with network configurations, attackers can create unmonitored pathways within the environment, conceal malicious activity, and expand their control over compromised cloud assets.
name: OCI Potential Network Security Tampering Attempt
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Oracle Cloud Infrastructure' and event.type in ('DeleteNetworkSecurityGroup', 'UpdateNetworkSecurityGroupSecurityRules', 'DeleteSecurityList', 'UpdateSecurityList') and unmapped.data.message contains ' succeeded'

description: Detects a successful brute force attack against Oracle Cloud Infrastructure (OCI) user accounts. This indicates that an attacker has repeatedly attempted authentication and eventually gained access using valid credentials. Such activity may lead to unauthorized access, privilege escalation, or further compromise of cloud resources. Monitoring this event is critical for identifying compromised accounts and preventing potential misuse within the OCI environment.
name: OCI Successful Brute Force Attack

description: Detects the deletion of a Virtual Network Interface Card (VNIC) in Oracle Cloud Infrastructure (OCI). Adversaries may delete VNICs to disrupt network connectivity, evade monitoring, or hinder incident response activities. Monitoring VNIC deletions is critical to identify potential malicious activity affecting network configurations and availability of cloud resources.
name: OCI VNIC Deleted
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Oracle Cloud Infrastructure' and event.type='DeleteVnic' and unmapped.data.message contains 'DeleteVnic succeeded'

description: Detects multiple failed login attempts followed by a successful login in Office 365, potentially indicating a successful brute force attack. An adversary may use brute force techniques to guess passwords, repeatedly attempting to log in until they succeed. This behavior is a strong indicator of unauthorized access attempts and can lead to compromised accounts, data breaches, and further exploitation of organizational resources.
name: Office 365 Successful Brute Force Attack

description: Detects multiple failed login attempts followed by a successful login to Palo Alto Networks GlobalProtect, potentially indicating a successful brute force attack. An adversary may use brute force techniques to systematically guess passwords, repeatedly attempting to log in until they succeed. This behavior is a strong indicator of unauthorized access attempts and can lead to compromised accounts, data breaches, and further exploitation of organizational resources.
name: PANW Firewall GlobalProtect Successful Brute Force Attack

description: Detects high and critical severity correlation events generated by Palo Alto Networks firewalls' automated correlation engine. The correlation engine connects isolated network events and looks for patterns that indicate a more significant event. This helps identify suspicious traffic patterns and network anomalies which, when correlated, indicate with a high probability that a host on the network has been compromised.
name: PANW Firewall High Severity Correlation Event Detected
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Palo Alto Networks Firewall' and metadata.log_name = 'CORRELATION' and unmapped.severity in ('high', 'critical')

description: Detects active network communication associated with known malware that is being allowed by the Palo Alto Networks firewall. This may indicate an ongoing security threat, where malicious traffic is bypassing firewall protections, potentially leading to system compromise, data exfiltration, or further infiltration within the network.
name: PANW Firewall Malware Allowed
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Palo Alto Networks Firewall' and metadata.log_name = 'THREAT' and unmapped.sub_type = 'virus' and not (unmapped.action contains ('deny', 'drop', 'reset', 'block') or action contains ('deny', 'drop', 'reset', 'block'))

description: Detects medium severity correlation events generated by Palo Alto Networks firewall's automated correlation engine. The correlation engine connects isolated network events and looks for patterns that indicate a more significant event. This helps identify suspicious traffic patterns and network anomalies which, when correlated, indicate with a high probability that a host on the network has been compromised.
name: PANW Firewall Medium Severity Correlation Event Detected
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Palo Alto Networks Firewall' and metadata.log_name = 'CORRELATION' and unmapped.severity = 'medium'

description: Detects a RDP brute force attempt followed by a successful rdp login as detected by Palo Alto Networks Firewall, indicating a potential brute force attack. An adversary may use brute force techniques to guess passwords, repeatedly attempting to log in until they succeed. This behavior is a strong indicator of unauthorized access attempts and can lead to compromised accounts, data breaches, and further exploitation of organizational resources.
name: PANW Firewall Successful RDP Brute Force Attack

description: Detects allowed network traffic to the TOR network. Adversaries can use TOR to anonymize their network activity, bypass security controls, and evade detection while conducting malicious operations. This could lead to unauthorized access, data exfiltration, and compliance violations if deemed malicious.
name: PANW Firewall TOR Traffic Allowed
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Palo Alto Networks Firewall' and metadata.log_name = 'TRAFFIC' and app_name = 'tor' and unmapped.action = 'allow'

description: Detects when the Palo Alto Networks firewall does not block traffic to a URL associated with malware distribution or operation. This typically indicates a lapse in the firewall's threat intelligence or a misconfiguration. An adversary can abuse this by using the unblocked URL to download malware onto a target system, establish a command and control channel, or exfiltrate data.
name: PANW Firewall Traffic to Malicious URL Allowed
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Palo Alto Networks Firewall' and metadata.log_name = 'THREAT' and unmapped.sub_type = 'url' and (unmapped.threat_category = 'malware' or unmapped.url_category = 'malware') and unmapped.severity in ('critical', 'high', 'medium') and not (unmapped.action contains ('deny', 'drop', 'reset', 'block'))

description: Detects when the Palo Alto Networks firewall does not block traffic to a URL known to be used in phishing attacks. An adversary can abuse this by directing victims to the phishing site, potentially stealing credentials, deploying malware, or conducting other malicious activities.
name: PANW Firewall Traffic to Phishing URL Allowed
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Palo Alto Networks Firewall' and metadata.log_name = 'THREAT' and unmapped.sub_type = 'url' and (unmapped.threat_category = 'malware' or unmapped.url_category = 'malware') and unmapped.severity in ('critical', 'high', 'medium') and not (unmapped.action contains ('deny', 'drop', 'reset', 'block'))

description: Detects unauthorized modifications to the Palo Alto Networks firewall configuration, potentially indicating malicious activity or security policy violations. These changes can be leveraged to evade detection, facilitate data exfiltration, or disrupt network defenses, ultimately compromising the integrity and security of the environment.
name: PANW Firewall Unauthorized Config Change
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Palo Alto Networks Firewall' and unmapped.type = 'CONFIG' and unmapped.result = 'Unauthorized'

description: Detects instances where the Program Compatibility Assistant (pcalua.exe) is executed via a scheduled task. Adversaries may exploit pcalua.exe, a signed Microsoft binary, to bypass User Account Control (UAC) and run commands with elevated privileges. This technique can facilitate persistence, such as leveraging a scheduled task to launch Python for malicious purposes, including Redcurl's Living off the Land (LOTL) tactics. Monitoring this activity is crucial for identifying unauthorized privilege escalation and persistent threats.
name: Pcalua Execution via Scheduled Task

description: Detects HTTP request patterns indicative of attempts to exploit the Tomcat RewriteValve path traversal vulnerability (CVE-2025-55752) to escape the web root and access arbitrary files. This behavior is security-relevant because successful exploitation can expose sensitive data and facilitate follow-on compromise of the application or host. Activity is commonly observed from external reconnaissance, automated exploit frameworks, or mass scanning infrastructure, and may occasionally originate from legitimate vulnerability assessment tooling.
name: Possible Tomcat RewriteValve Path Traversal Exploit Attempt
s1ql: (dataSource.name = 'Corelight' and http_request.http_method in:anycase ('GET', 'POST') and http_request.url.url_string contains ('/;/web-inf/', '%2fweb-inf%2f', '../web-inf/', '/;/meta-inf/', '%2fmeta-inf%2f', '../meta-inf/')) or (dataSource.name = 'AWS Web Application Firewall' and http_request.http_method in:anycase ('GET','POST') and web_resources.url.path contains ('/;/web-inf/', '%2fweb-inf%2f', '../web-inf/', '/;/meta-inf/', '%2fmeta-inf%2f', '../meta-inf/') and unmapped.action = 'ALLOW')

subQueries.subQuery: event.type = 'Process Creation' and (tgt.process.displayName contains ('Splashtop', 'AnyDesk', 'screenconnect', 'AteraAgent', 'rustdesk', 'TightVNC', 'UltraVNC', 'RealVNC') or tgt.process.displayName matches 'VNC.*(Server|Viewer)'  or tgt.process.name contains ('AnyDesk', 'AteraAgent', 'rustdesk.exe', 'vncserver', 'tvnserver', 'winvnc', 'vncviewer'))

event.type = 'Process Creation' and (tgt.process.displayName contains ('Angry IP Scanner', 'advanced port scanner', 'Network Scanner Application', 'Application for scanning networks', 'Advanced IP Scanner') or tgt.process.name contains ('advanced_port_scanner', 'ipscan.exe', 'netscan', 'advanced_ip_scanner'))

description: Detects the use of tooling combinations that suggest an adversary is conducting internal reconnaissance while establishing or enabling remote access capabilities. This pattern often indicates preparation for persistence or remote control following initial compromise. In typical environments, legitimate users do not scan the network while setting up remote access tools, making this activity highly unusual. The combination points to intent to maintain access or pivot within the network. Identifying this behavior provides early warning of potential lateral movement or long-term foothold establishment. It enhances defender visibility into threat actor staging tactics during the early phases of an intrusion.
name: Potential Adversary Tooling for Discovery and Remote Access

description: Detects instances where rundll32.exe is used to execute the DllRegisterServer export function, a behavior commonly associated with adversary tactics. Adversaries can abuse this functionality to execute malicious DLLs while blending in with legitimate system processes, evading detection by defenders. By leveraging rundll32.exe, attackers may bypass application whitelisting or gain persistence on the system by registering malicious components. This technique can also be used to load and execute payloads without leaving artifacts that directly implicate the malicious DLL, complicating forensic analysis and detection efforts. Such activity is often seen in attacks involving malware installation, lateral movement, or execution of scripts within a legitimate process.
name: Potential Application Bypass with DllRegisterServer Function
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (tgt.process.name in:anycase ('rundll32.exe') or tgt.process.displayName = 'Windows host process (Rundll32)') and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'DllRegisterServer' and not (src.process.user contains ('SYSTEM', 'AUTORITE NT', 'SISTEMA')) and not (tgt.process.cmdline contains 'silverlight')

description: Detects suspicious or unauthorized login attempts targeting Fortinet FortiGate firewalls, specifically linked to CVE-2024-55591 and the Console Chaos campaign. Adversaries exploit this vulnerability to gain administrative access, bypass authentication mechanisms, and compromise firewall security. Monitoring login activity is critical for detecting early signs of exploitation and preventing unauthorized access to network infrastructure.
name: Potential Fortinet FortiGate Unauthorized Login Attempt (CVE-2024-55591)
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'FortiGate' and unmapped.ui = 'jsconsole' and unmapped.user = 'admin' and unmapped.action = 'login' and (unmapped.srcip matches '^127(?:\\.\\d{1,3}){3}$' or unmapped.srcip matches '^(\\d{1,3})\\.\\1\\.\\1\\.\\1$')

description: Detects the presence of long hexadecimal-encoded sequences within command line arguments on Linux endpoints. Adversaries may leverage hexadecimal encoding to obfuscate malicious payloads, scripts, or commands, making them less recognizable to traditional security controls and static detection mechanisms. By embedding payloads in this format, attackers can bypass signature-based defenses and evade detection during initial access, privilege escalation, or lateral movement activities. Monitoring for such behavior can help identify sophisticated adversary tactics aimed at evading endpoint defenses and achieving persistence or further compromise within the environment.
name: Potential Hexadecimal Payload in Command Line
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'linux' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and tgt.process.cmdline matches '(\\\\x[0-9a-fA-F]{2}){20,50}'

description: Detects behavioral indicators of remotely started scheduled tasks with characteristics consistent with Impacket's atexec.py tool. This includes tasks with 8-character random names executed with System integrity level, often used for lateral movement and remote code execution.
name: Potential Impacket AtExec Scheduled Task Execution
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Behavioral Indicators' and indicator.name = 'ScheduledTaskProcessStartedRemotely' and indicator.metadata matches '(?i)Task\\s*Name:\\s*"\\\\[a-zA-Z]{8}"' and indicator.metadata contains 'IntegrityLevel: "System"' and not (src.process.cmdline contains ('Airwatch') or src.process.verifiedStatus = 'verified')

description: Detects potential use of PowerShell scripts or commands to load and manipulate image files from common writable directories, which may indicate steganography techniques used to hide or extract malicious content. Attackers leverage these methods to evade detection by embedding payloads within seemingly benign images. While such image processing can occur in legitimate contexts, monitoring this behavior helps identify suspicious activity that may signal malware execution or data concealment attempts.
name: Potential PowerShell Image Steganography
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Command Script' and src.process.displayName contains ('Windows PowerShell') and cmdScript.content contains ('[System.Drawing.Image]::FromFile', '[System.Drawing.Graphics]::FromImage', 'GetPixel', 'SetPixel') and cmdScript.content matches ('(\\\\Appdata|\\\\programdata|\\\\windows\\\\temp|%appdata%|%temp%).*\\\\[A-Za-b0-9-_]+\\.(png|jpeg|jpg|gif)')

description: Detects the use of string slicing techniques within PowerShell commands to obfuscate code execution. Adversaries employ this method to evade signature-based detection by reconstructing malicious commands at runtime. This behavior is indicative of advanced obfuscation tactics often observed in malware delivery, privilege escalation, or lateral movement scenarios. Monitoring for such patterns helps identify attempts to bypass security controls and execute unauthorized actions on target systems.
name: PowerShell Obfuscated Command Execution via String Slicing
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Command Script' and (src.process.name in:anycase ('powershell.exe', 'pwsh.exe', 'powershell_ise.exe') or src.process.displayName in ('Windows PowerShell', 'PowerShell_ISE')) and cmdScript.content matches '[a-zA-Z0-9\\-_]{50,}"\\)\\[[0-9\\s,]+\\]\\s*-join'

description: Detects instances where the Private Character Editor process spawns PowerShell or CMD child processes. This behavior is uncommon in legitimate workflows and may indicate User Account Control (UAC) bypass by adversaries. Threat actors may abuse trusted system utilities like Private Character Editor to launch command-line interpreters and evade traditional security controls. Such activity aligns with defense evasion and execution tactics observed in advanced attacks.
name: PowerShell or CMD Spawned by Private Character Editor Process
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (src.process.name in:anycase ('eudcedit.exe') or src.process.displayName in ('Private Character Editor')) and (tgt.process.name in:anycase ('powershell.exe', 'cmd.exe') or tgt.process.displayName in ('Windows PowerShell', 'Windows Command Processor'))

description: Detects possible process injection or processes attempting to masquerade as the legitimate Windows Error Reporting Service by imitating its name or characteristics. This behavior is indicative of adversaries leveraging trusted system processes to evade detection and blend malicious activity. Masquerading as, or injecting into, the Windows Error Reporting Service may allow attackers to bypass security controls, persist on the system, or execute unauthorized actions under the guise of a trusted process. Such activity aligns with known adversary tradecraft for defense evasion and privilege escalation.
name: Process Masquerading or Injecting into Windows Error Reporting Service
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type in ('Process Creation', 'Network Connection') and ((src.process.name in:anycase ('wermgr.exe', 'WerFault.exe') or src.process.displayName in ('Windows Problem Reporting')) or (tgt.process.name in:anycase ('wermgr.exe', 'WerFault.exe') or tgt.process.displayName in ('Windows Problem Reporting'))) and event.network.direction = 'OUTGOING' and not (dst.ip.address in ('127.0.0.1', '::1')) and event.network.protocolName != 'domain'

description: Detects the use of the dd utility to manipulate or corrupt the memory of a running process by writing directly to /proc/<pid>/mem. This technique, observed in attacks by UNC3886, can be used to patch live processes in memory—such as disabling logging or altering behavior—without restarting the process or leaving obvious traces on disk. Such activity is highly suspicious and may indicate advanced post-exploitation behavior or defense evasion tactics.
name: Process Memory Corruption via DD
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'linux' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and tgt.process.name = 'dd' and tgt.process.cmdline matches '/proc/\\d+/mem' and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'conv=notrunc' and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'oseek='

description: Detects attempts to export Windows Registry hives or keys known to store third-party credentials. This behavior may indicate credential harvesting by adversaries seeking to obtain sensitive authentication data from installed applications. Such activity is consistent with post-compromise tactics where attackers extract credentials for lateral movement or privilege escalation. Unusual registry export operations involving credential storage locations warrant further investigation for potential unauthorized access.
name: Registry Export Activity Targeting Third-Party Credential Storage
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (tgt.process.name in:anycase ('reg.exe') or tgt.process.displayName in ('Registry Console Tool')) and tgt.process.cmdline contains ('save', 'export') and tgt.process.cmdline contains ('\\Software\\Aerofox\\Foxmail\\', '\\Software\\Aerofox\\FoxmailPreview', '\\Software\\DownloadManager\\Passwords', '\\Software\\FTPWare\\COREFTP\\Sites', '\\Software\\IncrediMail\\Identities', '\\Software\\Martin Prikryl\\WinSCP 2\\Sessions', '\\Software\\Mobatek\\MobaXterm', '\\Software\\OpenSSH\\Agent\\Keys', '\\Software\\OpenVPN-GUI\\configs', '\\Software\\ORL\\WinVNC3\\Password', '\\Software\\Qualcomm\\Eudora\\CommandLine', '\\Software\\RealVNC\\WinVNC4', '\\Software\\RimArts\\B2\\Settings', '\\Software\\SimonTatham\\PuTTY\\Sessions', '\\Software\\SimonTatham\\PuTTY\\SshHostKeys', '\\Software\\Sota\\FFFTP', '\\Software\\TightVNC\\Server', '\\Software\\WOW6432Node\\Radmin\\v3.0\\Server\\Parameters\\Radmin')

description: Detects instances where Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) connections are enabled or allowed through the Windows Firewall using the netsh command. By modifying the firewall configuration, attackers can open RDP ports to facilitate lateral movement, maintain control over compromised hosts, or prepare the environment for further malicious activities.
name: Remote Desktop Enabled via Netsh
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (tgt.process.name contains 'netsh.exe' or tgt.process.displayName = 'Network Command Shell') and tgt.process.cmdline contains 'firewall add ' and tgt.process.cmdline contains ('localport=3389', 'RemoteDesktop', 'group=remote desktop') and tgt.process.cmdline contains ('action=allow', 'enable=Yes') and not (src.process.user contains ('SYSTEM', 'AUTORITE NT', 'SISTEMA'))

description: Detects network connections initiated by remote monitoring and management tooling to suspicious top-level domains. This behavior can indicate adversaries leveraging remote administration capabilities to establish command-and-control, stage follow-on actions, or exfiltrate data while blending into legitimate remote access activity. Correlating RMM-originated egress with anomalous domain characteristics helps surface covert external communications commonly used during post-compromise operations. Adversaries frequently abuse RMM channels to maintain access and control while masking traffic within expected management workflows.
name: RMM Tool Network Traffic to Suspicious Top-Level Domains
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'DNS Resolved' and ((src.process.name matches ('screenconnect\\..*client.*\\.exe', 'meshagent.*.exe') OR src.process.name in:anycase ('AnyDesk.exe', 'TeamViewer.exe', 'TeamViewer_Service.exe', 'client32.exe', 'rustdesk.exe', 'AteraAgent.exe', 'SRManager.exe', 'SRFeature.exe', 'SRAgent.exe', 'SRFeatureSOS.exe', 'SRAppPB.exe', 'SRServer.exe', 'SRVirtualDisplay.exe', 'TacticalRMM.exe', 'rmm-agent.exe', 'Action1_Agent.exe', 'pdq-connect-agent.exe') OR src.process.image.originalFileName matches ('screenconnect\\..*client.*\\.exe', 'meshagent.*.exe') OR src.process.image.originalFileName in:anycase ('AnyDesk.exe', 'TeamViewer.exe', 'TeamViewer_Service.exe', 'client32.exe', 'rustdesk.exe', 'AteraAgent.exe', 'SRManager.exe', 'SRFeature.exe', 'SRAgent.exe', 'SRFeatureSOS.exe', 'SRAppPB.exe', 'SRServer.exe', 'SRVirtualDisplay.exe', 'tacticalrmm.exe', 'rmm-agent.exe', 'Action1_Agent.exe', 'pdq-connect-agent.exe') OR src.process.displayName contains ('screenconnect', 'AnyDesk', 'TeamViewer Remote Control Application Installer', 'rustdesk', 'meshagent', 'AteraAgent', 'Splashtop® Streamer SRManager', 'Tactical RMM', 'PDQ Connect Agent') OR src.process.publisher contains ('ANYDESK SOFTWARE', 'PHILANDRO SOFTWARE', 'SPLASHTOP INC.')) OR (tgt.process.name matches ('screenconnect\\..*client.*\\.exe', 'meshagent.*.exe') OR tgt.process.name in:anycase ('AnyDesk.exe', 'TeamViewer.exe', 'TeamViewer_Service.exe', 'client32.exe', 'rustdesk.exe', 'AteraAgent.exe', 'SRManager.exe', 'SRFeature.exe', 'SRAgent.exe', 'SRFeatureSOS.exe', 'SRAppPB.exe', 'SRServer.exe', 'SRVirtualDisplay.exe', 'tacticalrmm.exe', 'rmm-agent.exe', 'Action1_Agent.exe', 'pdq-connect-agent.exe') OR tgt.process.displayName contains ('screenconnect', 'AnyDesk', 'TeamViewer Remote Control Application Installer', 'rustdesk', 'meshcentral', 'AteraAgent', 'Splashtop® Streamer SRManager', 'Tactical RMM', 'PDQ Connect Agent') OR tgt.process.publisher contains ('ANYDESK SOFTWARE', 'PHILANDRO SOFTWARE', 'SPLASHTOP INC.'))) and event.dns.request matches ('\\.xyz$', '\\.top$', '\\.site$', '\\.info$', '\\.sh$', '\\.ru$', '\\.su$', '\\.icu$', '\\.pw$', '\\.ga$', '\\.club$', '\\.work$', '\\.click$', '\\.rest$', '\\.shop$', '\\.fun$')

description: Detects unattended registration of GitHub self-hosted runners with suspicious characteristics associated with the Shai-Hulud worm variant.
name: Shai-Hulud Unattended GitHub Runner Registration
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and ((src.process.cmdline contains 'github.com' and src.process.cmdline contains '--unattended' and src.process.cmdline contains '--name SHA1HULUD') or (tgt.process.cmdline contains 'github.com' and tgt.process.cmdline contains '--unattended' and tgt.process.cmdline contains '--name SHA1HULUD'))

description: Detects the scheduled task of SSH connections utilizing the ProxyJump option with multiple hops, indicating potential lateral movement or covert tunneling activity. This behavior may be leveraged by adversaries to bypass network segmentation and access internal systems through chained SSH proxies. The use of multi-hop SSH ProxyJump connections can facilitate stealthy command execution and complicate detection efforts. Such activity aligns with tactics commonly observed in advanced persistent threat operations seeking to evade network defenses.
name: SSH ProxyJump Multi-Hop Scheduled Task
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.category = 'scheduled_task' and event.type in:anycase ('Task Start', 'Task Register') and ((src.process.cmdline matches '(?:\\s|^)ssh(\\.exe)?(?:\\s|$)' and (src.process.cmdline matches:matchcase '(?:\\s|^)\\-J(?:\\s|$)' or src.process.cmdline contains ('ProxyJump=', 'ProxyCommand=') and not (src.process.cmdline contains 'ProxyJump=none'))) or (task.path matches '(?:\\s|^)ssh(\\.exe)?(?:\\s|$)' and (task.path matches:matchcase '(?:\\s|^)\\-J(?:\\s|$)' or task.path contains ('ProxyJump=', 'ProxyCommand=') and not (task.path contains 'ProxyJump=none'))))

description: Detects a remote command via WinRM (wsmprovhost.exe) creating a scheduled task to establish a persistent reverse SSH tunnel. This is a common adversary pattern for creating a durable, firewall-bypassing C2 backdoor.
name: SSH Reverse Tunnel Persistence via Remote PowerShell

description: Detects SSH sessions that establish reverse tunnels through known public tunneling services. This behavior is commonly used by adversaries to bypass inbound firewall restrictions, create covert remote access channels, and maintain control of internal assets from the internet. Such activity may also occur in environments where administrators or developers legitimately use public tunneling services for remote access or testing.
name: SSH Reverse Tunnels to Public Tunneling Services
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and event.type in ('Process Creation', 'Command Script') and ((tgt.process.cmdline matches '(?:\\s|^)ssh(\\.exe)?(?:\\s|$)' and tgt.process.cmdline matches:matchcase '(?:\\s|^)\\-R(?:\\s|$)' and (tgt.process.cmdline matches:matchcase '(?:(?:\\s|^)\\-o(?:\\s|$)|(?:\\s|^)\\-N(?:\\s|$))' or tgt.process.cmdline contains 'StrictHostKeyChecking=no') and tgt.process.cmdline matches '(?i)\\b(serveo\\.net|ngrok\\.com|ngrok\\.io|localhost\\.run|localtunnel\\.me|pagekite\\.net|tunnelto\\.dev|tunnel\\.us|sshreach\\.me|ssh\\.localhost\\.run|ssh\\.pagekite\\.net|ssh\\.tunnelto\\.dev|forward\\.ssh\\.com|expose\\.dev|bore\\.pub|telebit\\.io|frp\\.fun|inlets\\.dev|cloudflared\\.net|playit\\.gg|remote\\.it|webhookrelay\\.com|portmap\\.io|ssh-j\\.com|ssi\\.sh|connect\\.ngrok-agent\\.com|connect\\.eu\\.ngrok-agent\\.com|connect\\.ap\\.ngrok-agent\\.com|connect\\.jp\\.ngrok-agent\\.com)\\b') or (cmdScript.content matches '(?:\\s|^)ssh(\\.exe)?(?:\\s|$)' and cmdScript.content matches:matchcase '(?:\\s|^)\\-R(?:\\s|$)' and (cmdScript.content matches:matchcase '(?:(?:\\s|^)\\-o(?:\\s|$)|(?:\\s|^)\\-N(?:\\s|$))' or cmdScript.content contains 'StrictHostKeyChecking=no') and cmdScript.content matches '(?i)\\b(serveo\\.net|ngrok\\.com|ngrok\\.io|localhost\\.run|localtunnel\\.me|pagekite\\.net|tunnelto\\.dev|tunnel\\.us|sshreach\\.me|ssh\\.localhost\\.run|ssh\\.pagekite\\.net|ssh\\.tunnelto\\.dev|forward\\.ssh\\.com|expose\\.dev|bore\\.pub|telebit\\.io|frp\\.fun|inlets\\.dev|cloudflared\\.net|playit\\.gg|remote\\.it|webhookrelay\\.com|portmap\\.io|ssh-j\\.com|ssi\\.sh|connect\\.ngrok-agent\\.com|connect\\.eu\\.ngrok-agent\\.com|connect\\.ap\\.ngrok-agent\\.com|connect\\.jp\\.ngrok-agent\\.com)\\b'))

description: Detects the establishment of an SSH reverse tunnel to a domain address. Reverse SSH tunnels allow a machine behind a firewall or NAT to provide access to its local services by initiating an outbound SSH connection to an external server, effectively bypassing inbound network restrictions. While this technique can be used legitimately for secure remote administration, adversaries may exploit it to maintain persistent access to compromised systems, exfiltrate data, or facilitate lateral movement within a network. description: Detects unauthorized or unusual file write operations targeting the SharePoint 'Layouts' directory. This directory is a critical component of SharePoint's web application infrastructure and is frequently targeted by adversaries seeking to introduce malicious web shells or tamper with application behavior. Unauthorized modifications in this location may indicate an attempt to gain persistence, escalate privileges, or facilitate lateral movement within the environment. Such activity aligns with known tactics where threat actors exploit web application directories to maintain access or execute arbitrary code.
name: Suspicious File Write Activity in SharePoint Layouts Directory
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type in ('File Creation', 'File Rename', 'File Modification') and (src.process.name in:anycase ('cmd.exe', 'powershell.exe', 'pwsh.exe') or src.process.displayName in ('Windows Command Processor', 'Windows PowerShell')) and tgt.file.path contains ('Program Files\\Common Files\\Microsoft Shared\\Web Server Extensions\\', 'Program Files (x86)\\Common Files\\Microsoft Shared\\Web Server Extensions\\') and tgt.file.path contains '\\TEMPLATE\\LAYOUTS\\' and (tgt.file.extension in:anycase ('asax', 'ascx', 'ashx', 'asmx', 'asp', 'aspx', 'bat', 'cmd', 'cer', 'config', 'hta', 'jsp', 'jspx', 'php', 'ps1', 'vbs'))

description: Detects the creation of a scheduled task where the task name or associated binary mimics svchost.exe, a legitimate Windows system process. Adversaries often use masquerading techniques to make malicious tasks appear benign, reducing the likelihood of detection during manual inspection or automated analysis. Identifying such suspicious scheduled task creations is critical to uncovering persistence mechanisms and evasion techniques.
name: Suspicious Scheduled Task Masquerading as Svchost
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and event.type = 'Task Register' and (src.process.name in:anycase ('schtasks.exe') or src.process.displayName = 'Task Scheduler Configuration Tool') and src.process.cmdline contains 'svchost.exe'

description: Detects the registration of a scheduled task on a Windows system that mimics the name of a legitimate system process (e.g., “Windows Update Security”, “Microsoft Telemetry”) while referencing suspicious file paths such as AppData, Temp, ProgramData, or Startup. This technique is commonly used by adversaries to establish persistence while evading user suspicion by masquerading as trusted components or services. Monitoring for such activity can help identify early signs of compromise or malware persistence.
name: Suspicious Scheduled Task Masquerading as Windows Service
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os == 'windows' and event.type == 'Task Register' and (src.process.name = 'schtasks.exe' or src.process.displayName = 'Task Scheduler Configuration Tool') and task.name matches '(?i)(Windows\\s+Update\\s+Security.*|Windows\\s+Security.*|Windows\\s+Defender.*|Security\\s+Patches.*|System\\s+Maintenance.*|Microsoft\\s+Telemetry.*)' and src.process.cmdline matches '(?i)(programdata|appdata|temp|public|downloads|users|tmp|startup|recycle\\.bin|debug|fonts|inetpub|windows\\help|perflogs|roaming)'

description: Detects the registration of a scheduled task using a PowerShell script file with suspicious characteristics, such as uncommon file paths, obfuscated content, or known malicious patterns. While task registration via PowerShell can be legitimate, adversaries often leverage this technique to establish persistence or automate malicious activities. Monitoring this behavior helps identify potential abuse of task scheduling for unauthorized or harmful purposes.
name: Task Registered via Suspicious PowerShell Script File
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Task Register' and src.process.name = 'powershell.exe' and src.process.activeContent.path matches '.*\\.(log|txt|dat|csv|inf|cfg|ini|json|xml)$' and src.process.activeContent.signedStatus = 'unsigned' and not(src.process.activeContent.path contains 'ZscalerFix.xml')

description: This rule detects system-level actions related to user and group account management on Linux endpoints. Such operations may include the creation of new user accounts, modification of existing accounts or their credentials, and adjustments to group memberships. While these activities are common in administrative workflows, they can also indicate malicious behavior when performed unexpectedly or by unauthorized users. Adversaries may leverage these actions to establish persistence, escalate privileges, or maintain covert access within the environment.
name: User Management Event
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'linux' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and tgt.process.name in ('useradd', 'adduser', 'usermod', 'passwd', 'chpasswd', 'groupadd', 'groupmod', 'gpasswd', 'deluser', 'userdel') and not (tgt.process.cmdline contains (' -h', ' --help')) and not (tgt.process.name = 'passwd' and tgt.process.cmdline contains (' -S ', ' --status')) and not (tgt.process.cmdline matches 'useradd -D(\\s|$)')

description: Detects the creation of scheduled tasks that match characteristics typically associated with the Impacket tool, including specific commands found in the task content. Attackers often use tools like atexec.py from Impacket to create stealthy, system-scheduled tasks that execute payloads or commands on remote systems. These tasks allow adversaries to maintain persistence, execute arbitrary code, or spread laterally within a network. Extended Windows Event Log collection must be enabled for this rule to work properly.
name: WEL Potential Impacket Scheduled Task Creation
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'Windows Event Logs' AND winEventLog.id = 4698 AND winEventLog.data.event.eventData.taskName matches "^\\\\[A-Za-z]{8}$" AND winEventLog.data.event.eventData.taskContent contains '/c ' and winEventLog.data.event.eventData.taskContent matches '\\\\[A-Za-z]{8}\\.tmp'

description: Detects 100 or more failed login attempts followed by a successful login, indicating a potential brute force attack. An adversary may use brute force techniques to guess passwords, repeatedly attempting to log in until they succeed. This behavior is a strong indicator of unauthorized access attempts and can lead to compromised accounts, data breaches, and further exploitation of organizational resources.
name: WEL Successful Brute Force Attack

description: Detects the removal of the Windows Defender context menu handler from the Windows Registry using reg.exe or PowerShell. This activity may indicate an attempt to weaken endpoint security controls or evade detection by disabling quick access to Windows Defender features. Adversaries may leverage this technique to reduce the likelihood of their actions being discovered or interrupted by security tools. Such behavior is consistent with defense evasion tactics observed in targeted attacks.
name: Windows Defender Context Menu Handler Registry Removed
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type in ('Process Creation', 'Command Script') and (src.process.name in:anycase ('powershell.exe', 'pwsh.exe', 'powershell_ise.exe', 'reg.exe') or src.process.displayName in:anycase ('Windows PowerShell', 'pwsh', 'PowerShell ISE', 'Registry Console Tool')) and (src.process.cmdline contains ('del', 'Remove-Item', 'ri ') or cmdScript.content contains ('del', 'Remove-Item', 'ri ')) and (src.process.cmdline contains '\\shellex\\ContextMenuHandlers\\EPP' or cmdScript.content contains '\\shellex\\ContextMenuHandlers\\EPP')

description: Detects execution of commands leveraging the 'SystemSettingsAdminFlows' LOLBin to disable Windows Defender. This behavior is indicative of adversaries attempting to weaken endpoint security controls by abusing legitimate system utilities. Such activity aligns with common defense evasion tactics, enabling threat actors to bypass protections and facilitate further malicious actions. Monitoring for this technique is critical, as it may precede additional compromise or lateral movement within the environment.
name: Windows Defender Disable via LOLBin SystemSettingsAdminFlows
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type in ('Process Creation', 'Command Script') and (src.process.name in:anycase ('powershell.exe', 'cmd.exe') or src.process.displayName in:anycase ('Windows PowerShell', 'Windows Command Processor')) and (src.process.cmdline contains 'Defender' and src.process.cmdline matches:matchcase 'DisableEnhancedNotifications\\s+1|RTP\\s+1|SpynetReporting\\s+0|SubmitSamplesConsent\\s+0') or (cmdScript.content contains 'Defender' and cmdScript.content matches:matchcase 'DisableEnhancedNotifications\\s+1|RTP\\s+1|SpynetReporting\\s+0|SubmitSamplesConsent\\s+0')

description: Detects modification of Windows Defender 'WdFilter' and Tamper Protection registry keys. Such changes may indicate attempts to disable or weaken built-in security controls, enabling adversaries to evade detection and maintain persistence. Threat actors often target these registry settings to bypass Windows Defender protection mechanisms as part of defense evasion tactics. Unauthorized alterations to these keys can significantly reduce the effectiveness of Windows Defender, increasing the risk of further compromise.
name: Windows Defender WdFilter and Tamper Protection Registry Alteration

description: Detects the use of netsh.exe to disable the local Windows firewall. Attackers can exploit this technique to weaken the security posture of the system, allowing for unrestricted network communication. Disabling the firewall can facilitate lateral movement, enable command and control (C2) activity, and allow malicious traffic to flow undetected.
name: Windows Firewall Disabled via Netsh
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (src.process.name in:anycase ('netsh.exe') or src.process.displayName='Network Command Shell') and src.process.cmdline contains 'advfirewall' and src.process.cmdline contains ' state ' and src.process.cmdline contains ' off' and not (src.process.parent.cmdline contains:anycase ('\\Symantec\\Symantec Agent', '\\EmpirumAgent\\', '\\VMwareHorizonOSOptimizationTool', '\\OmnissaHorizonOSOptimizationTool'))

description: Detects WinRAR file extraction activities that leverage path traversal combined with alternate data streams to write files outside the intended extraction directory. This technique can be used by adversaries to bypass directory restrictions and deposit malicious files in sensitive locations. Exploiting alternate data streams in conjunction with path traversal may indicate an attempt to evade security controls or establish persistence. Such behavior aligns with tactics used to escalate privileges or facilitate lateral movement within a compromised environment.
name: WinRAR Path Traversal File Write via Alternate Data Streams
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Behavioral Indicators' and indicator.name = 'WriteToADS' and (src.process.name in:anycase ('winrar.exe', 'winrar') or src.process.displayName in ('WinRAR archiver', 'WinRAR.exe', 'WinRAR')) and indicator.metadata contains '\\..\\'

description: Detects WMI queries to enumerate installed antivirus products, commonly used by adversaries to identify security software before deploying malware or exploits. This technique allows attackers to adjust their tactics to evade detection.
name: WMI AntiVirus Product Enumeration
s1ql: dataSource.name = 'SentinelOne' and endpoint.os = 'windows' and event.type = 'Process Creation' and (src.process.name in ('cmd.exe') or src.process.displayName = 'Windows Command Processor') and src.process.cmdline contains 'Namespace' and src.process.cmdline contains 'root' and src.process.cmdline contains 'SecurityCenter2' and src.process.cmdline contains 'AntiVirusProduct'
